question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a BlockRequest with a PartialBlockIdentifier where Index and Hash refer to different blocks, causing RetrieveBlock() to resolve one block's transactions under another block's identity, reorganizing which transactions appear at which block height?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a BlockRequest with only a Hash set (Index nil) to force RetrieveBlock() to call FindByHash() instead of FindByIdentifier(), and if hash normalization via SafeRemoveHexPrefix() is inconsistent, cause a different block's transaction set to be returned under a spoofed block identity?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a BlockRequest with only Index set (Hash nil) to force RetrieveBlock() to call FindByIndex(), and if the index-to-block mapping is not validated against a canonical hash, cause transactions from a reorganized fork to appear as the canonical block at that height?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a BlockRequest with both Index and Hash as nil, causing RetrieveBlock() to call RetrieveLatest(), and by racing concurrent requests during a block transition, cause the latest block's transaction list to be inconsistently assembled from two different blocks' ConsensusStartNanos/ConsensusEndNanos ranges?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the maxTransactionsInBlock truncation logic (block.Transactions = block.Transactions[0:s.maxTransactionsInBlock]) to cause certain transactions to be silently moved to the otherTransactions list, effectively hiding them from the primary transaction view and reorganizing the visible transaction history of a block?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user, by querying a block with exactly maxTransactionsInBlock transactions, cause the boundary condition in the slice operation (block.Transactions[0:s.maxTransactionsInBlock]) to include or exclude a boundary transaction differently across concurrent requests, leading to inconsistent transaction ordering in the block response?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that otherTransactions only contains TransactionIdentifier.Hash (not full transaction data) to cause a block response where the primary transaction list and otherTransactions list present conflicting orderings of the same transaction set, reorganizing the apparent history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where FindBetween() returns transactions ordered differently than the canonical consensus order due to a missing ORDER BY clause in the underlying query, causing block.Transactions to present a reorganized transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a BlockRequest targeting a block whose ConsensusStartNanos equals the ConsensusEndNanos of the previous block, causing FindBetween() to include a boundary transaction in two different blocks simultaneously, reorganizing which block that transaction belongs to?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the inclusive range semantics of FindBetween(ctx, block.ConsensusStartNanos, block.ConsensusEndNanos) to cause a transaction at exactly ConsensusStartNanos or ConsensusEndNanos to appear in multiple blocks, creating a duplicated transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: BlockTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a BlockTransactionRequest where BlockIdentifier.Index and BlockIdentifier.Hash refer to different blocks, causing FindByIdentifier() to resolve one block's ConsensusStartNanos/ConsensusEndNanos range, then FindByHashInBlock() to locate a transaction that belongs to a different block, effectively placing a transaction in the wrong block?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: BlockTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a TransactionIdentifier.Hash that matches a transaction outside the resolved block's ConsensusStartNanos/ConsensusEndNanos window, and if FindByHashInBlock() does not strictly enforce the timestamp boundary, cause a transaction to appear as belonging to a different block than its actual consensus block?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: BlockTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the SafeRemoveHexPrefix() call on BlockIdentifier.Hash in BlockTransaction() to craft a hash value that, after prefix stripping, matches a different block than intended, causing FindByIdentifier() to return a block with a different ConsensusStartNanos/ConsensusEndNanos range and thus misattribute a transaction to the wrong block?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: BlockTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a BlockTransactionRequest with a TransactionIdentifier.Hash that uses different hex casing or prefix variants (e.g., '0X' vs '0x') to bypass hash normalization in SafeRemoveHexPrefix(), causing FindByHashInBlock() to fail to find the canonical transaction and instead return a different transaction, reorganizing the apparent block contents?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: updateOperationAccountAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user poison the entityCache (LRU cache keyed by accountId.GetId()) by triggering GetAccountAlias() to cache a stale or incorrect alias for an account ID, causing subsequent Block() or BlockTransaction() calls to return operations with wrong account identifiers, reorganizing the apparent ownership of transactions in block history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: updateOperationAccountAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the LRU cache eviction policy (lru.WithCapacity(entityCacheConfig.MaxSize)) by flooding requests with many distinct account IDs to evict a legitimate cached alias, then trigger a re-fetch that returns a different alias due to a race condition, causing block operations to show incorrect account identities?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: updateOperationAccountAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the cache key collision in entityCache where s.entityCache.Set(result.GetId(), result) uses the resolved account's encoded ID as key, but the original accountId.GetId() may differ, causing a future cache lookup to return the wrong AccountId for a different account's operations, reorganizing transaction attribution?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: updateOperationAccountAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a race condition between concurrent Block() requests where one goroutine reads from entityCache while another is writing via s.entityCache.Set(), causing operations in a block response to have inconsistently mixed account aliases, presenting a reorganized view of transaction ownership?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: updateOperationAccountAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the entityCache to serve a cached alias for an account that has since been deleted or reassigned, by querying a block before and after an account deletion event, causing the block response to show the old alias and misrepresent the transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: updateOperationAccountAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that updateOperationAccountAlias() modifies operations[index].AccountId in-place on the shared block.Transactions slice, and if the same Transaction object is referenced by multiple concurrent requests, cause a data race that corrupts the AccountId of operations, reorganizing transaction attribution across concurrent block responses?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a BlockRequest with a negative block Index to cause RetrieveBlock() to call FindByIndex() with a negative value, and if the repository does not validate negative indices, cause an unexpected block (e.g., genesis or a wrapped index) to be returned with its transactions presented under the requested index?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a BlockRequest with a block Index of 0 to cause RetrieveBlock() to return the genesis block, and if the genesis block's ParentIndex/ParentHash self-references are not validated, cause a client to enter an infinite loop traversing the block chain, effectively disrupting the apparent chain structure?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ToRosetta() conversion in block.ToRosetta() where ParentHash is prefixed with SafeAddHexPrefix(), and if the stored ParentHash is already hex-prefixed, cause a double-prefixed hash to be returned, making the parent block unreachable by hash lookup and breaking the chain linkage?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a BlockRequest with a Hash containing only the '0x' prefix and no actual hash bytes, causing SafeRemoveHexPrefix() to produce an empty string, and if FindByHash() or FindByIdentifier() treats an empty hash as a wildcard, return an unintended block with its transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/block_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Block()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a BlockRequest with an extremely large block Index (e.g., MaxInt64) to cause RetrieveBlock() to call FindByIndex() with an overflow value, and if the repository wraps the index, return a block from a completely different part of the chain with its transactions presented under the attacker-chosen index?
